coredata.dc:title: Bio-route synthesis of carbon quantum dots from tulsi leaves and its application as a draw solution in forward osmosis
coredata.dc:creator: Doshi, Krushangi
Mungray, Alka A.
coredata.dc:description: Forward osmosis (FO) is one of the emerging membrane technologies which has gained interest for being a low energy process. The important factors controlling the FO process are membrane, draw solution (DS) and its recovery. In this research paper, a novel draw solution based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was synthesized using Tulsi (Ocimumtenuiflorum). 5 % concentration CQDs were prepared via two different hydrothermal methods i.e. autoclave (A-1) and reflux (R-1). Prepared CQDs were physically characterized by fluorescence spectra, DLS, TEM, XRD, FT-IR and biologically analysed by minimum inhibitory concentration. To prevent microbial growth and to obtain higher water flux, 25 % and 50 % glycerol is added in prepare CQDs. Among both, 50 % glycerol is effective in inhibit microbial growth. Hence, prepared 5 % CQDs of R-1 method with 50 % glycerol is termed as “TCQD-G” and this used for all experimental work as a novel draw solution. TCQD-G performance was investigated and compared with 1 M NaCL in FO with DI water. The prepared draw solute TCQD-G was also used in FO with synthetic wastewater as feed. Maximum water flux achieved by draw solution i.e., TCQD-G and 1 M NaCl was 10.72 L/m2h (LMH) and 8.002 LMH, respectively for DI water as a feed solution. The highest water flux achieved using synthetic waste water as a feed solution was 5.34 LMH with TCQD-G as DS. Reverse solute flux (RSF) was observed 0.03 g/m2h (gMH) and 0.13 gMH for TCQD-G and 1 M NaCl draw solutions, respectively for DI water as feed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Draw solution
Tulsi leaves
Carbon quantum dots
Forward osmosis
Bio-route

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Water Flux evaluation of prepared draw solution
Reverse solute flux determination of prepared draw solution

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Actual image of FO setup in batch mode.
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of Tulsi CQDs at different wavelength (B) excitation wavelength spectra (C) CQDs under visible and UV light.
DLS profile of prepared 5 % concentration tulsi CQDs by autoclave(A-1) and reflux (R-1) method.
(A) TEM image of prepared tulsi CQDs (B) SAED image of prepared tulsi CQDs.
XRD graph of synthesized tulsi CQDs with two different methods.
FTIR spectra of synthesized tulsi CQDs (a) A-1 (b) R-1.
Tulsi draw solution prepared using autoclave (A-1) and reflux method (R-1) with different concentration.
Tulsi CQDs plated with different concentration (A) reflux method (B) autoclave.
Addition of (A) 25 % and (B) 50 % glycerol with 5 % tulsi draw solution from R-1.
Comparison of water flux and reverse solute flux (RSF) of 1 M NaCl solution and TCQD-G solution with DI water as feed.
Water flux of TCQD-G solution using synthetic waste water as a feed solution.
Composition of synthetic waste water and its parameter.
Comparison study of FT-IR peaks of Tulsi CQDs.
Comparison of Water flux and RSF of different draw solution.

head.ce:title: Bio-route synthesis of carbon quantum dots from tulsi leaves and its application as a draw solution in forward osmosis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Department of Chemical Engineering, Surat, 395007, Gujarat, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Department of Chemical Engineering, Surat, 395007, Gujarat, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Forward osmosis (FO) is one of the emerging membrane technologies which has gained interest for being a low energy process. The important factors controlling the FO process are membrane, draw solution (DS) and its recovery. In this research paper, a novel draw solution based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was synthesized using Tulsi (Ocimumtenuiflorum). 5 % concentration CQDs were prepared via two different hydrothermal methods i.e. autoclave (A-1) and reflux (R-1). Prepared CQDs were physically characterized by fluorescence spectra, DLS, TEM, XRD, FT-IR and biologically analysed by minimum inhibitory concentration. To prevent microbial growth and to obtain higher water flux, 25 % and 50 % glycerol is added in prepare CQDs. Among both, 50 % glycerol is effective in inhibit microbial growth. Hence, prepared 5 % CQDs of R-1 method with 50 % glycerol is termed as “TCQD-G” and this used for all experimental work as a novel draw solution. TCQD-G performance was investigated and compared with 1 M NaCL in FO with DI water. The prepared draw solute TCQD-G was also used in FO with synthetic wastewater as feed. Maximum water flux achieved by draw solution i.e., TCQD-G and 1 M NaCl was 10.72 L/m2h (LMH) and 8.002 LMH, respectively for DI water as a feed solution. The highest water flux achieved using synthetic waste water as a feed solution was 5.34 LMH with TCQD-G as DS. Reverse solute flux (RSF) was observed 0.03 g/m2h (gMH) and 0.13 gMH for TCQD-G and 1 M NaCl draw solutions, respectively for DI water as feed.

ce:section.ce:para: Today in this fast-growing world freshwater existence is getting limited [1]. Different sources i.e., municipal waste water, dairy water, brackish water are indentified and accordingly various methods includes desalination, membrane distillation, electrolysis are used to recover fresh water [2–4]. Commercially Reverse osmosis (RO) membrane processes are used for the generation of potable water [5] but it has several limitations such as high cost, rapid fouling, and high energy requirement [6]. To overcome these limitations, Forward Osmosis (FO) came into account. Forward osmosis is a phenomenon where the transfer of water takes place due to the osmotic pressure gradient. Two important key factors for the processing of FO are ideal FO membrane and efficient draw solution (DS). In FO, the concentration of DS plays a vital role [7]. Ideal DS should have higher osmotic pressure, non-toxic, high solubility, low reverse solute flux (RSF), compatible with membrane and easy recovery [8]. In previous studies, inorganic salts such as NaCl, MgCl2, KCl, CaCl2, MgSO4 and (NH4)2CO3 and organic molecules such as ethanol, sugar and polyelectrolytes with various concentration were mostly used as draw solutes in FO applications due to their high availability, high solubility and low cost [9]. However, still, limitations like recovery of draw solutes from diluted solution is costly along with excess reverse salt diffusion towards feed water for such draw solutes cause hindrance.

During FO process, diffusion of draw solutes towards feed side may reduce the net osmotic driving force and can cause membrane fouling through internal concentration polarization and feed solution contamination [10]. In one study, it was observed that ammonium bicarbonate and magnesium chloride caused the toxic effect to E. coli in FO membrane bioreactor which was due to the accumulation of draw solute to the feed solution [11]. In one sudy, glycerol was applied as a polar organic based DS in forward osmosis application [12]. Magnetic nanoparticles were also explored as a novel draw solute because of its surface modification, which can be recovered by magnetic field. Magnetic nanoparticles face several problems such as low water flux and aggregation of particles [13]. In literature, one study explored water-soluble polyelectrolyte solution poly sodium acrylate (PSA) as a draw solute for seawater desalination using FO with initial water flux of 5 LMH. Another invention was hydrophilic polymer such as hydrogel which was first tested for desalination by directly immersing it into seawater [14]. Various responsive draw solutions such as stimuli-responsive polymer hydrogels [15] and switchable polarity solvents [10] have also been explored to overcome reverse salt leakage and recovery problem but suffers with poor long-term stability [16]. Nowadays nanoparticles play a very vital role because of its surface passivating properties. Theoretically, DS prepared using nanoparticles are well dispersed in water and possess very low reverse solute diffusion. The surface of nanoparticles catches ionic species which helps in increasing osmotic pressure of DS. Most nanoparticles used as a DS have paramagnetic property. Researcher group performed FO experiments with prepared from Fe3O4 as a DS [17,18].

A new and fascinating class of nanostructure as a form of carbon quantum dots have taken in interest since last decades. With the size range of less than 10 nm carbon quantum dots (CQDs) contain rich surface chemistry, chemical inertness, biocompatibility, hydrophilicity including surface passivation properties. The aqueous dispersion of CQDs demonstrates high osmotic pressure and which allows high water flux. CQDs have been explored in a wide range of application such as drug delivery, diagnostics, sensing, bioimaging, energy storage [20,21]. So far now in literature Na+ functionalized carbon quantum dots prepared from citric acid generates acceptable result as a DS against seawater desalination [19]. Na+ functionalized DS possesses higher osmotic pressure of 37 atm with higher water flux of around 28.9 LMH at 0.5 g/L of concentration of CQDs. Due to the rich ionic species present in CQDs their aqueous dispersion may provide high chemical inertness, biocompatibility and hydrophilicity.

Tulsi is an herbal plant which is easily available, inexpensive and is having medicinal value. It is vastly used for the treatment of several ailments including ozena, skin diseases, gastric and various stress-induced disorders. These characteristics are due to the variety of functional moieties present within it. Functional groups such as alcohols, aldehydes and ketones are rich in carbon, nitrogen and oxygen species that can be tailored onto the surface of carbon dots thereby preventing the use of any additional passivating agent. In the past several years many research groups have used Tulsi CQDs in various applications like bio-imaging, sensing, patterning etc. [22,23]. Hence, Tulsi leaves act as an excellent precursor for the preparation of CQDs. Considering the above discussion, there is a surge for the effective draw agent for making FO process commercially feasible.

Therefore, in this paper 5 % concentration of CQDs are synthesised from tulsi leaves as a draw solute by hydrothermal method (autoclave (A-1) and reflux (R-1)). Prepared tulsi CQDs are characterized for its physical properties assessment by DLS, TEM, XRD, FT-IR. For its biological properties assessment, MIC was performed. Prepared draw solution, 5 % CQDs from R-1 method was optimized for further study based on its lower size. 50 % glycerol was added to prepare CQDs, to prevent microbial growth and to obtain higher water flux (TCQD-G). As per best of author’s knowledge, TCQD-G is very first time utilized as draw solution (DS) in FO. Forward osmosis efficiency was investigated and compared via water flux and reverse solute flux (RSF) with two different DS i.e., TCQD-G and 1 M NaCl with DI water as feed. Also, draw solute TCQD-G effectiveness was investigated with synthetic wastewater as feed. This study may shed some valuable insight on the next generation draw solution and its application as a natural draw solution for FO technology.

Fresh Tulsi leaves were obtained from the garden of our campus and were washed thoroughly with Mili-Q water before use. Glycerol and NaCl were purchased from Rankem, India. All the chemicals procured were of analytical grade. Throughout the experiment and for making solution Mili-Q ultrapure water was used.

There are various methods available for the preparation of carbon quantum dots. Here we followed the top-down, facile, economic and green hydrothermal treatment method for the preparation of CQDs from Tulsi leaves. Here in this work, we had tried two different methods for comparison.

Method-1 (further denoted as A-1) In this procedure 2.5 gm of fresh Tulsi leaves were finely chopped into small pieces and 35 mL of Milli-Q water was added. The mixture was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon reactor followed by hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 4 h in an autoclave. After completion of the reaction, the vessel was allowed to cool down at room temperature and the aqueous suspensions were filtered using a membrane containing 0.22 μm pore size in order to remove large particles. The dispersed fine particles of CQDs having yellowish colour was obtained in the supernatant after filtration. This supernatant had an effective concentration of 5 mg/mL and was stored at 4 °C till further use [22]. CQDs were diluted with DI water to prepare different ratios solution.

Method-2 (further denoted as R-1) Himaja et al. prepared the quantum dots using peels of kitchen waste [24]. In this physically modified method, the pulp of fresh leaves was prepared using an electric mixer. 70 gm pulp of tulsi leaves was prepared and mixed in 250 mL of distilled water. This mixture was then refluxed at 150 °C for 2 h using round bottom flask. The temperature was maintained by circulating cold water continuously. Reflux solution was cooled at room temperature and filtered using a regular household tea filter resulting in a yellow coloured solution. Then this solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min to remove any heavier particles present in the solution. Decantation technique was used to obtain the working solution. Centrifuged Solution had a clear pale-yellow colour supernatant solution. The same procedure was followed twice. The final product was filtered using a membrane containing 0.22 μm pore size membrane filtration system with a concentration of 5 mg/mL. A clear and bright yellow suspension of CQDs was obtained. CQDs were diluted with DI water to prepare different ratios solution.

The fluorescence property of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) was characterized under UV- light at 365 nm [25]. Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed to check its excitation property using Perkin Elmer fluorescence spectrometer. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) was used to measure the hydrodynamic size of nanoparticles using Malvern Zetasizer 2.0 [26]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed by drop-casting the sample onto copper grids and imaging was conducted using PHILIPS CM-200 operated at 200 kV [16]. The wide-angle X-ray diffraction pattern was recorded in the range of 0°-80° using MiniFlex-2 (Cu-Kα radiation) with the scan rate of 1°/min [22]. The Infrared spectra (FT-IR) was recorded on Alpha-II, Germany using KBr pellets. The Minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) test was performed to test bacterial growth in the presence of CQDs at different dilution [15]. The overnight grown bacterial cell culture of E. coli was used for MIC testing. The bacterial culture was spread plated on a nutrient agar plate. The dilutions were loaded on Whatman 1 paper disc (50 μL). These discs were incubated on the same spread plated agar plates at 37 °C overnight for 12 h. Prepared Tulsi CQDs using two different methods which (Autoclave and Reflux methods) were diluted with different concentration such as 5 %, 10 %, 15 % and 20 % to check the antibacterial properties.

H-type set up was used for the construction of the FO unit made up of glass. The unit comprised of cylindrical feed and draw chamber having a capacity of 500 mL of total volume in each chamber. Forward osmosis experiments were performed in batch mode as shown in Fig. 1 . Each chamber had an opening of 2.5 cm diameter to fit the flange coupling (rubber sheet stuck to the flanges to hold the membrane properly). Thin-film composite FO (TFC-FO) membranes developed by HTI Inc. (Hydration Technology Innovations, LLC, Albany, OR, USA) were used in experimental setup due to their high-water permeability as compared to the cellulose tri-acetate (CTA) based HTI-FO membranes [27]. The effective area of the membrane was 4.91 × 10−4 m2. Membranes were soaked in deionized water (DI) water overnight before the experiment to remove the preservative and pre wet the pore structure.

Experiments of FO were performed and compared with DI water as a feed solution and TCQD-G and 1 M NaCl as a draw solution in batch mode. Forward osmosis performance was investigated by water flux and RSF. TCQD-G were also tested with synthetic wastewater as feed solution. Composition of synthetic wastewater is given in Table 1 .

The COD (Chemical oxygen demand) was measured using close reflux method. Dissolved oxygen (DO), pH and conductivity were measured using different probes with automatic temperature compensation (HACH, USA).

Where A is the pure water permeability, Δπ and ΔP are osmotic and hydraulic pressure differences between two solutions. For FO, Δ P is zero. Experimentally Jw in Lm-2 h−1 (abbreviated as LMH) was calculated using the change in volume of the feed reservoir, Δ V ; the membrane area, Am
and change in time t.
(2)
J w =
Δ V
A m
t
;

The salt leakage rate or reverse salt diffusion Js in gm-2h-1 (abbreviated as gMH) from the draw solution to the feed side was determined from the increase of the feed conductivity
(3)
J s =
Δ (
C t
V t
)
Δ t
A m
where Ct and Vt are the salt concentration and the volume of the feed solution at the end of the FO test respectively [27].

Tulsi leaves contain a large number of organic molecules such as rosmarinic acid, oleanolic acid, ursolic acid, carvacrol and many more [29]. Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) are generally defined as small nanoparticles which come in the range of 1−10 nm. CQDs possess some unique properties in the form of surface passivation. The CQDs were synthesized by green one-step hydrothermal method using tulsi leaves as a raw material. These organic molecules converted into CQDs by hydrothermal treatment using autoclave and Reflux system. The CQDs had come up into focus because of its high solubility, low toxicity, superficial synthesis, low raw material and preparation cost, photostability and great biocompatibility [30–32]. The prepared tulsi CQDs showed strong green emission under ultraviolet light at 365 nm. Based on robust properties of CQDs we can employ them as a draw solution in the FO system. The prepared tulsi CQDs were used as a DS for water flux and RSF calculation.

CQD particles have a special characteristic of photo luminance. This property has been observed in various CQDs such as orange peel, aloe-vera etc. in the emission wavelength of 350−470 nm [20]. It was reported that the excitation wavelength was 450 nm. The prepared CQDs were also tested for the range of 310–440 nm shown in Fig. 2 (A, B). The optical property of synthesized CQDs was investigated using fluorescence spectroscopy shown in Fig. 2. In this work, the maximum intensity of fluorescence was observed at 440 nm and excitation wavelength was 422 nm which is relatable to the reference. At 422 nm wavelength, maximum particles get excited. This intense peak was due to the π-π* electronic transition of C = C and n-π* transition of C = O present on CQDs surface. Fig. 2(C) represents a photographic image of aqueous CQDs under visible light and emission under 365 nm. From the image, it can be observed that the aqueous solution gives a bright green colour under the emission of UV light which implies the formation of CQDs. The observed green colour confirms the formation of fluorescence CQDs from tulsi leaves [22].

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) is a technique that can be used to determine the size distribution profile of small suspended particles based on their hydrodynamic size. Fig. 3 shows the size distribution by number percentage which concludes the average hydrodynamic size of nanoparticles of 33.4 nm and 27.1 nm for autoclave and reflux sample respectively. From the DLS histogram, it is clearly seen that the hydrodynamic size of R-1 sample is low compare to A-1. Therefore, all the experiments will be performed with 5 % concentration of prepared tulsi carbon quantum dots from reflux method (R-1). The hydrodynamic size revealed by DLS is slightly more than the mean dried-state diameter illustrated in TEM may be due to the agglomeration of the particles. In another study, the hydrodynamic size of tulsi CQDs has observed 70 nm [26].

The morphology (disperse formation and shape) of nanoparticles was also analysed by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). Fig. 4 (A) shows the size of particles which is approximately 5−7 nm. TEM image revealed uniform black dots particles which are well dispersed in water and the shape of particles seems spherical. This particular characterization ensures the formation of CQDs. This data in line with the prepared tulsi CQDs in literature who observed particle size and shape of CQDs with TEM [22]. Fig. 4(B) shows the Selected area diffraction (SAED) spectrum of prepared tulsi CQDs. Form SAED spectrum we can observe that the plane formation which shows the amorphous nature of the CQDs which can be further confirmed by XRD.

The degree of crystallinity of CQD was determined by XRD. In Fig. 5 , XRD pattern shows diffraction peaks around 2θ = 28.21° and 40.25° for A-1 and 28.44° and 40.60° for R-1, which correspond to (002) plane with an interlayer d spacing of 0.318 nm and (100) with d spacing of 0.21 nm respectively. Both the XRD graph of A-1 and R-1 shows the same plane which represents the tulsi CQDs contain almost the same diffraction peaks. This represents the presence of closely packed carbon atoms with surface functionality. There is always a requirement of capping of nanoparticles to protect it from agglomeration. Capping agent covers the refine surface of nanoparticles and prevents them from coagulation or aggregation [33]. In our study, we used glycerol as a capping agent which could further help us in sustaining the particles from getting agglomerated and inhibiting biological growth.

Glycerol is a naturally occurring chemical which is generally used in medicine, food contains for weight losing, healthcare purpose, etc [34]. Moreover, glycerol has been used as a base fluid to suspend nanoparticles [35]. Here to explore the stability against microbial growth we tried with glycerol. Glycerol acted as a good agent for draw solution in FO also [36,37]. To prevent the prepared CQDs from agglomeration and microbial inhabitation glycerol solution was analysed. Glycerol was added with varying weight ratios i.e., 25 % and 50 % in 5 mL of 5 % concentration of prepared tulsi CQDs from R-1 method to check its stability and antimicrobial properties. Fig. 9 (A) indicates dilution in 25 % with glycerol and Fig. 9(B) for 50 % dilution with glycerol. 25 % dilution indicates microbial growth in Fig. 9(A) while using 50 % dilution, no microbial growth was observed. So, for further application in FO, 50 % dilution with glycerol with 5 % concentration of R-1 prepared tulsi CQDs solution was taken as a draw solution. This is denoted as “TCQD-G” in full paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water Flux evaluation of prepared draw solution
ce:section.ce:para: The main driving force of osmotically driven membrane process is an osmotic gradient between the feed solution and draw solution which is separated by the semi-permeable membrane. The osmotic pressure engrossed by the draw solution should be high enough to produce high water travel across the membrane [28]. To generate high osmotic pressure, draw solutes should be highly soluble in water to achieve a high concentration of dissolved ions. Conductivity and osmotic pressure are positively related to each other. Conductivity and osmotic pressure both measure the concentration of chemical ionic species present in the draw solution [38]. The FO experiment was performed using 1 M NaCl solution and synthesized TCQD-G as a draw solution using commercially available HTI membrane. This FO setup was operated for 40 h for 1 M NaCl and synthesized TCQD-G as a draw solution. The initial conductivity of 1 M NaCl was measured as 68.94 mS/cm. Consequently, the initial conductivity of TCQD-G was observed as 72.296 mS/cm which is a bit higher than the NaCl solution. Higher conductivity represents the presence of a greater number of ionic species in the prepared CQDs draw solution which proportionally increases the concentration.

Based on the data given in Table 3 , water flux and reverse solute flux of two different draw solutions can be compared. Initial water flux was observed as 8.002 LMH for 1 M NaCl which is comparable from the data given in Table 3 for the researcher as per the reference [7]. For TCQD-G, initial water flux was 10.72 LMH (Fig. 10 ) which is quite high compare to reference [7] as given in Table 3. Average water flux of 1 M NaCl solution and TCQD-G for 40 h was calculated as 4.729 LMH and 9.90 LMH respectively. Compare to 1 M NaCl, prepared TCQD-G possess abundant amount of ionic species, higher conductivity and concentration, therefore, TCQD-G gave higher water flux compare to 1 M NaCl. If we compare our experimental data with reference [19] where Na+ functionalized CQDs was taken, it gave higher water flux compares to present work. This may be due to the additional functional group of Na+ present on the surface of CQDs which enhanced the water flux but Na+ functionalized CQDs were prepared by using chemicals. In this study the synthesized draw solution was prepared directly using natural precursor Tulsi which leads to the application in drinking purpose just by single post-filtration. Here, we can also observe that the water flux was also declined with increasing time. Membrane fouling due to the boundary layer formation on the membrane surface in batch mode may be the reason for this trend. Another reason for this declined flux is the continuous dilution of draw solution which can reduce the concentration gradient as well as osmotic pressure and therefore flux. With DI water, TCQD-G as DS has gave higher water flux compare to NaCl. Therefore, in continuation of this, determination of water flux was done using synthetic was water as a feed solution against TCQD-G as DS. The highest water flux achieved using synthetic waste water as a feed solution was 5.34 LMH shown in Fig. 11 . At the beginning of the experiment draw solution showed marginally higher flux as a result of higher osmotic potential. After certain period of operation gradually decline of flux is noticeable due to the concentration polarization of large number of organic molecule present in the feed side of the membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reverse solute flux determination of prepared draw solution
ce:section.ce:para: Reverse solute flux is inevitable by a semi-permeable membrane. Based on this, membranes are not considered as ideal barriers. RSF was calculated because it could be the reason for feed solution contamination and draw solute loss [39]. Fig. 10 also shows the reverse solute flux (gMH) plot for 1 M NaCl and TCQD-G with increasing time i.e., up to 40 h. The initial RSF of 1 M NaCl was observed as 0.13 gMH and for TCQD-G it was 0.03 gMH which increased with increasing time. Compared to NaCl solution, the RSF of our draw solution (TCQD-G) is quite negligible. The final RSF observed was 0.3 and 0.12 for 1 M NaCl and synthesized TCQD-G respectively which shows that salt solution has higher RSF value. Compared to Na+ functionalized CQD, we can say that we obtained RSF value nearly same which is near to negligible. RSF of draw solute can change the feed solution chemistry thus enhance membrane fouling, which extends to the rate of reverse solution diffusion and its ability to interact with the foulant. From the above experiment, it was observed that the prepared draw solution gave higher water flux compared to 1 M NaCl solution with HTI membrane. Reverse solute flux valve is very low i.e., 0.03 gMH for TCQD-G with DI water as feed. Due to reparation of TCQD-G is bio-route based, it will not give any adverse effect to feed solution. Therefore, we have not investigated RSF data for synthetic wastewater as feed.

In this work, we have devised a direct, green synthetic approach for synthesizing CQDs using tulsi leaves without using any additional passivating agent for surface modification. Herein, we applied the one-pot hydrothermally synthesized CQDs from tulsi leaves via two methods autoclave (A-1) and reflux (R-1) to prepare 5 % concentration solution. Prepared CQDs were characterized via fluorescence spectra, DLS, TEM, XRD, FT-IR and minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC). To prevent microbial growth and to obtain higher water flux, 50 % glycerol was added in prepare CQDs from R-1 method, named as TCQD-G. These CQDs has an abundant amount of carbonaceous species which helps in enhancing the conductivity and osmotic pressure and used as DS in FO application. This novel draw solution in FO operation gave higher water flux (10.72 LMH) and lower RSF (0.03 gMH) with DI water as feed compare to 1 M NaCl. Also, with synthetic wastewater, TCQD-G gave maximum water flux i.e., 5.34 LMH. Overall, we have demonstrated the probable use of TCQD-G for a myriad of practical future applications.

Dr. Alka A Mungray conceptualized the objectives, designed the study, reviewed and finalized the manuscript. Krushangi Doshi, synthesized the Tulsi CQDs, performed all experiments, interpreted the results and wrote the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge DST-SERB project (EEQ/2017/000814) entitled “The near future air cathode Osmotic microbial fuel cell (OMFC) for wastewater treatment, electricity generation and pure water extraction” for financial support for carrying out research work.
